Her growing impatience over the course of a session correlates with growing clarity about what she wants.
1.context: This insight applies broadly across Dora's AI interactions, particularly during extended late-night work sessions where she is deeply immersed in academic writing or technical tasks and her messages become increasingly terse.
supporting_evidence: Dora works continuously from 2:30 AM to 6:45 AM on academic paper revision (ID: 1020, 1021), demonstrating deep sustained commitment despite the terseness of her individual messages.

Early in sessions she asks for help generating content, but by later exchanges she's supplying her own draft sentences and directing the AI to simplify or match her framing (ID: 1014, 1015) — the shift from consumer to director reveals that her growing impatience correlates with growing clarity.

She sends an apparently empty message and still receives a response (ID: 1011), yet is simultaneously doing careful work ensuring SIGACCESS compliance for accessibility — hasty mechanics coexist with meticulous standards.

Her 'ehhh' and 'no brainstorm' responses (ID: 1017) read as dismissive, but they follow exchanges where she's engaging with precise technical distinctions about recursive restructuring and architectural gaps (ID: 1018, 1022) — she knows exactly what she wants and can instantly recognize when the output misses it.

1.title: Friction from AI's Failure to Recognize Generative vs. Critical-Evaluative Modes
1.tagline: Dora's relationship with AI shifts dramatically between generative and critical-evaluative modes, and the friction she experiences stems from the AI treating both situations with the same verbosity and scaffolding.
1.insight: When Dora needs rote output — code, figure descriptions, formatting — she wants the AI to be a silent, fast executor. When she's refining arguments, she wants the AI to be a precise intellectual partner. The AI treats both situations similarly, with explanation, scaffolding, and verbosity, creating friction in the first mode (where overhead is waste) and insufficient depth in the second (where she needs genuine engagement). The mismatch between her current cognitive mode and the AI's uniform response style is a central source of her frustration.
1.context: This insight applies whenever Dora switches between mechanical tasks (code generation, figure descriptions, formatting) and intellectually demanding tasks (argument refinement, study design, conceptual differentiation) within her AI-assisted workflows.
supporting_evidence: For figure descriptions, Dora submits near-identical prompts in rapid succession and pastes raw LaTeX directly (ID: 1009, 1013), treating the task as mechanical batch processing where any conversational overhead is waste.
For code generation, she bypasses all scaffolding with 'just give me the python code' (ID: 1005, 1007), signaling she needs execution, not education.

For argumentative refinement, she engages in extended back-and-forth, asks probing meta-questions about defensibility (ID: 1019), and iteratively tightens claims across multiple exchanges (ID: 1023) — here she wants depth, not speed.

For study design, she sends multiple follow-up messages progressively sharing new constraints (ID: 1029) and pushes back on impractical solutions (ID: 1034), showing she values genuine problem-solving engagement — a stark contrast to her 'just give me the code' moments.

1.title: Clear Internal Standards, Externalized Final Decisions
1.tagline: Dora knows exactly what 'good' looks like but relies on AI to produce or validate the final version rather than committing to it herself.
1.insight: Dora consistently arrives at AI interactions with a well-formed mental model of the desired output — she knows the right length, format, and structure. However, she externalizes the last mile of decision-making, deferring to AI tools to generate or confirm the final version. She rejects verbosity instantly and narrows scope decisively, yet hesitates to trust her own creative or editorial judgment when it comes to producing outputs from scratch. This creates a tension where she is highly opinionated about results but simultaneously uncertain about her ability to arrive at them independently.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is producing written content, formatting outputs, or making editorial choices — such as writing descriptions, selecting examples for presentations, or deciding how to present statistics on slides.
supporting_evidence: Dora requested a shorter description and stopped iterating the moment it matched her pre-existing expectation (ID: 1039, 1036), indicating she already had a clear internal benchmark.
She specified exact column headers for her tables mid-session (ID: 1055), revealing she had a formatting vision all along but used AI to execute it.
When choosing an example for her presentation or deciding how to format a statistic on a slide, she deferred to ChatGPT rather than trusting her own judgment (ID: 1048, 1049, 1058, 1059).

1.title: Speed-Driven Iteration Creates Compounding Failures
1.tagline: Dora's rapid-fire interaction style generates a paradoxical feedback loop where efficiency-seeking behavior produces cascading errors and inefficiency.
1.insight: Dora moves through problems at extraordinary speed, often with only seconds between messages and immediate code execution. However, this velocity prevents her from carefully absorbing AI responses before testing them, leading to cascading errors that a brief pause could have avoided. Her terse prompts often lack sufficient context for the AI to respond accurately, resulting in mismatched answers that require further correction. The efficiency she seeks is paradoxically undermined by a cycle of imprecise prompts, mismatched answers, errors, and yet more prompts.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is debugging code, working through technical problems, or engaging in any rapid iterative workflow where she submits prompts and immediately acts on AI responses.
supporting_evidence: Dora applied a `.tolist()` suggestion to what was actually a string (not a numpy array), hit a `malformed node` error just 5 seconds later, and had to course-correct (ID: 1043, 1045).

Her 9-second correction — 'but it's a string of a list' — revealed the AI's initial answer didn't match her actual situation, likely because her own prompt was too terse to convey the necessary context (ID: 1043, 1045).

The pattern of seconds between messages and instant follow-ups indicates she does not pause to verify AI outputs before executing them, creating a fragile workflow where small failures compound quickly.

1.title: Transactional Delegation Over Conversational Collaboration
1.tagline: Dora treats AI as a parallel co-worker she delegates to in directive bursts, experiencing the tool's clarifying questions as friction rather than collaboration.
1.insight: Across every observed session, Dora's interactions with AI are strikingly transactional: she issues short, directive commands, ignores or bypasses clarifying questions, and moves on the moment she receives a usable output. Her mental model of AI is closer to a command-line interface or a pre-briefed human assistant than to a conversational partner. She never engages in back-and-forth dialogue about the reasoning behind formatting choices — only that they should be made. This means the AI's attempts at iterative clarification, which are central to its design, actually slow her down.
1.context: This insight applies broadly across all of Dora's AI interactions — whether she is formatting data, generating content, or debugging — particularly when the AI attempts to offer multiple options or ask clarifying questions.
supporting_evidence: Dora dismissed Claude's multi-option suggestions with terse one-liners (ID: 1053), showing she views extended dialogue as unnecessary friction.
She submitted five separate data blocks in sequence like items on an assembly line (ID: 1051), reinforcing a delegation-in-bursts pattern rather than a collaborative exchange.
She never engaged in back-and-forth dialogue about why something should be formatted a certain way — only that it should be (ID: 1052, 1054), indicating a purely directive interaction style.

1.title: Seamless Task-Switching With Hidden Context Gaps
1.tagline: Dora fluidly pivots between cognitively distinct tasks without transitional friction, but each abrupt shift creates an unacknowledged context gap for the AI.
1.insight: Within single working sessions, Dora pivots abruptly between cognitively distinct tasks — from lighthearted tweet generation to research-based slide formatting, from debugging Python errors to writing presentation scripts. These transitions happen without any warm-up, context-setting, or framing for the AI tool. While this seamlessness reflects how deeply AI is embedded in Dora's workflow rhythm, it places a burden on the AI, which has no way to infer her shifting intent, audience, or stakes. The same tool is asked to serve as entertainment, coding assistant, writing coach, and data formatter within minutes, all without contextual cues.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is working across multiple projects or task types in a single session — particularly when she shifts between casual and professional tasks, or between creative and technical work.
supporting_evidence: Dora pivoted from lighthearted tweet generation to research-based slide formatting within a single session (ID: 1057), with 27 minutes of casual tweets followed by an immediate pivot to serious work content.
She transitioned from debugging Python errors to writing presentation scripts (ID: 1040, 1046) and from data table formatting to content ideation (ID: 1050, 1048) without any transitional framing.
These abrupt role shifts — entertainment, coding assistant, writing coach, data formatter — all occurred within minutes, with Dora providing almost no contextual framing for any of these transitions.

1.title: AI as a Sparring Partner, Not an Authority
1.tagline: Dora uses AI as a thinking partner she argues with, not as an authority she defers to.
1.insight: Dora consistently treats AI-generated outputs as rough drafts to be corrected, redirected, and overwritten rather than as finished products. She has a precise mental model for her work and becomes frustrated when AI misrepresents her intent — yet she keeps returning to AI tools to iterate. This suggests she values AI as a sounding board that externalizes her thinking, but the real intellectual labor remains firmly hers.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is producing intellectual or professional deliverables such as academic prose, stakeholder analyses, or presentation slides — any context where she has a strong preexisting mental model of what the output should convey.
supporting_evidence: After ChatGPT generates prose, Dora returns approximately 26 minutes later with her own partially rewritten version incorporating her own phrasing, then explicitly corrects ChatGPT's interpretation of stakeholder categories (ID 1065).
Dora pushes back directly, stating 'No those are directly involved or use it are model developers and end users,' redirecting ChatGPT to match her conceptual framework (ID 1069).
Dora rejects slide title suggestions twice through successive corrections, narrowing ChatGPT toward her intended communicative goal (ID 1071).
She is described as 'not passively accepting ChatGPT's outputs but actively correcting and redirecting them' (ID 1064).

1.title: Guarding Authentic Voice While Leaning on AI
1.tagline: Dora is deeply concerned about AI outputs eroding her authentic voice — even as she relies heavily on AI to produce that voice.
1.insight: There is a striking tension in Dora's behavior: she depends on AI to help her articulate things, yet she is simultaneously vigilant about the output not sounding like it came from AI. She wants AI to serve as invisible scaffolding — powerful enough to shape her communication but undetectable in the final product. This manifests in multiple revision cycles and explicit requests to de-automate the tone of generated text.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is drafting professional or academic communications such as cold outreach emails or other writing where perceived authenticity and personal voice are critical to the recipient's impression.
supporting_evidence: Dora explicitly asks ChatGPT to 'make it sound less LLM-ey,' directly flagging that AI-generated language is a liability in professional academic outreach (ID 1075).
She goes through at least three revision cycles on a single short cold email, iterating toward a tone that feels right (ID 1073).
Dora asks ChatGPT 'how do I say' twice, relying on AI to find the right words — the very words she then needs to sound authentically hers (ID 1079).
She is described as 'likely aware that her emails risk sounding AI-generated and cares about coming across as authentic and human' (ID 1074).

1.title: Fragmented Workflow, Deep Focus Within Each Fragment
1.tagline: Dora operates in a fragmented, high-context-switching workflow but maintains remarkable depth within each fragment.
1.insight: Despite toggling between wildly different tasks — academic prose, Python scripting, email outreach, slide design, statistical methodology — across sessions that span hours and stretch past 3am, Dora achieves deep analytical focus within each burst. Her workflow looks scattered from the outside due to rapid pivots, multi-hour gaps, and multiple deliverables, but each individual thread shows progressive, methodical deepening rather than superficial skimming.
1.context: This insight applies broadly across Dora's work sessions, especially when she is managing multiple concurrent projects or deliverables that require different cognitive modes, such as balancing research analysis with professional communications.
supporting_evidence: Within 6 minutes, Dora shifts from summarizing a 137-page PDF to requesting a Python script — two completely different cognitive tasks (ID 1061).
A roughly 4.5-hour gap separates HCLLM prose writing from slide title work, followed by another pivot back to HCLLM diagrams (ID 1067).

Over approximately 14 hours, she asks a tightly scoped sequence of progressively deeper technical questions — from PCA/UMAP to proportion tests to entropy to compositional data analysis — without detours (ID 1083).
At nearly 3am, she composes and refines two personalized outreach emails in a single session, each tailored to a specific researcher's work (ID 1077).

1.title: Delegation Instinct Meets a Quality Gap
1.tagline: Dora treats AI as a delegation layer to preserve her energy for conceptual work, but this creates a quality gap she then has to close manually.
1.insight: Dora's instinct is to offload execution — she asks for scripts, email drafts, analysis code, and even prompts to hand to another AI agent. But the outputs consistently fall short of her standards, pulling her back into hands-on refinement. She wants to operate at the strategic and conceptual level, but the current state of AI tooling means she ends up doing double work: first delegating, then fixing.
1.context: This insight applies when Dora is attempting to offload technical or execution-heavy tasks to AI, such as code generation, data analysis scripting, or drafting prompts for other AI agents, and expects production-ready results.
supporting_evidence: Just 11 seconds after receiving code, Dora asks for a prompt to hand to a coding agent — she wants to offload execution entirely (ID 1087).
After receiving the initial Python script, she immediately follows up asking to remove commented-out sections — the first output wasn't production-ready (ID 1063).
She recognizes a confound in her analysis ('the overall # of IWAs is just also decreasing'), prompting a methodological pivot that ChatGPT hadn't anticipated (ID 1085).
She exhibits 'iterative dissatisfaction or perfectionism' with script output, wanting it cleaner than what AI initially provides (ID 1062).

1.title: Rigor Without the Lecture
1.tagline: Dora values methodological rigor but wants it delivered as ready-to-run implementation, not theory.
1.insight: Dora operates in a tension between wanting to do things right and wanting to move fast. She proactively raises sophisticated concerns that demonstrate genuine critical thinking about validity, yet she consistently rejects extended theoretical discussion in favor of code she can paste and run immediately. This suggests she carries an internalized standard of rigor but experiences friction when the path to meeting that standard requires her to slow down and wade through explanation. The most effective responses for her package rigor directly into executable code.
1.context: When Dora asks technical or statistical questions, especially when she shares her own code and asks for enhancements or corrections.
supporting_evidence: Dora unpromptedly asks about Bonferroni correction before anyone suggests it, showing she is actively thinking about statistical validity (ID: 1088, 1089).
She pastes her actual code and says 'add significance testing' rather than asking for an explanation of the underlying theory (ID: 1091).
When Claude gives a lengthy multi-language response, Dora immediately pastes her code snippet within approximately five seconds rather than engaging with the explanation (ID: 1101).
She is task-focused and wants to move from concept to working implementation without theoretical discussion (ID: 1090).

1.title: Requirements Discovered Through Doing
1.tagline: Dora discovers what she actually needs through doing, not planning — her requirements emerge reactively as she hits real constraints.
1.insight: Dora doesn't arrive at conversations with a fully formed specification. Instead, she works in tight loops of trying, encountering friction, and then refining her ask. Her real requirements only surface after she's already in motion — running code, switching contexts, or hitting unexpected behavior. This means the first answer she receives is almost never the last answer she needs, and the most useful guidance would anticipate the constraints she hasn't yet discovered.
1.context: When Dora initiates a new technical task or asks a seemingly straightforward question — her initial ask is likely just the starting point of an iterative refinement process.
supporting_evidence: Dora asks about logger.info, pastes code, then 50 minutes later surfaces a completely new context (Jupyter notebook), then immediately narrows further with 'only print for my package though' — all discovered in sequence, not upfront (ID: 1103, 1102).
She receives Linux-focused scrolling advice, then clarifies she's on Windows SSHing into Linux — she didn't know the distinction mattered until the advice didn't fit (ID: 1099, 1098).
She runs Claude's code within approximately two minutes, hits a TypeError immediately, and pastes the traceback — her workflow is try-first, debug-second (ID: 1093, 1092).

Her presentation scripting follows the same pattern: she sends partial drafts, asks 'does this make sense,' corrects misunderstandings, and refines iteratively rather than drafting a complete outline first (ID: 1109, 1108).

1.title: Terse with Tools, Thoughtful with People
1.tagline: Dora is highly sensitive to tone and human impact in interpersonal communication, even while being terse and efficiency-driven in her own interactions with AI.
1.insight: There's a striking contradiction between how Dora communicates with AI tools — rapid, clipped, sometimes single-sentence corrections — and how carefully she considers how her communications will land on other people. When drafting a disqualification message for a study participant, her immediate instinct was to soften it and make it more empathetic. Her terseness with AI isn't rudeness — it's efficiency with a tool — while she reserves emotional labor and care for human-facing communication.
1.context: When Dora asks for help drafting messages, emails, or any communication intended for other people, especially in sensitive or professional contexts.
supporting_evidence: After Claude drafts the disqualification message, Dora's immediate follow-up is to request a 'more empathetic' tone, showing her concern for how the recipient will feel (ID: 1114, 1115).

Her presentation work shows concern for framing and accuracy — she corrects ChatGPT when it mischaracterizes her contribution and pushes for specifics on the Play Pumps failure (ID: 1106, 1107, 1110, 1111).
Yet with AI she is terse: single-line corrections, pasting code without preamble, following up within seconds with narrow constraints (ID: 1100, 1101, 1097).

1.title: Broad-to-Narrow Funnel Across Many Domains
1.tagline: Dora context-switches rapidly across many domains but consistently funnels from broad exploration to narrow, actionable decisions within each task.
1.insight: Across a single observation window, Dora moves between statistical analysis, terminal configuration, Python logging, Spotify API exploration, presentation scripting, participant communication, and documentation tooling. Despite this breadth, within each domain she follows a remarkably consistent pattern: she starts with a broad or general question, then rapidly narrows through follow-ups until she reaches a concrete, actionable decision point. This funnel pattern repeats so reliably that it appears to be her fundamental mode of learning and decision-making.
1.context: When Dora begins exploring a new topic or tool — expect her to start broad and quickly drill down through a series of follow-up questions toward a specific decision.
supporting_evidence: In documentation tooling, Dora moves from a broad question to a VitePress vs Read the Docs comparison, then asks for alternatives, and finally anchors on GitHub Pages hosting (ID: 1116, 1117).

With the Spotify API, she asks about OAuth, then redirect URIs, then the recently-played endpoint, then full history, then how stats.fm works — all within approximately three minutes (ID: 1112, 1113).
For terminal scrolling, she moves from garbled output to how to scroll to clarifying her Windows+SSH environment (ID: 1094, 1095, 1096, 1099).
Her presentation follows the same funnel: sends draft, asks if it makes sense, corrects a misunderstanding, then pushes for more specificity (ID: 1109, 1107, 1111).

1.title: Context Mismatch Breaks Trust Instantly
1.tagline: When AI responses don't match Dora's actual context, she loses trust in the current thread and pivots quickly rather than trying to adapt the answer herself.
1.insight: Dora doesn't try to translate mismatched advice to her situation — she flags it and resets. Whether it's Linux instructions when she's on Windows, a mischaracterization of her research contribution, or a too-vague explanation, Dora treats context mismatches as signals that the AI doesn't understand her situation and immediately corrects course. Contextual accuracy is a prerequisite for her trust, and a single mismatch can undermine the perceived value of an entire response.
1.context: When providing Dora with technical guidance, environment-specific instructions, or any response that references her work or situation — accuracy of context is critical to maintaining her engagement.
supporting_evidence: Dora quickly clarifies 'I am on a windows computer but ssh into linux' after receiving Linux-only advice, rather than trying to adapt the instructions herself (ID: 1099, 1098).
She corrects ChatGPT within 19 seconds when it frames her paper as prior work rather than her own contribution, showing how quickly she detects and flags context errors (ID: 1107, 1106).
When Claude's code throws an error on the very first run, she pastes the full traceback immediately rather than attempting to debug independently (ID: 1093, 1092).
She pushes back on a general Play Pumps summary, wanting specifics that match the level of detail her presentation requires (ID: 1110, 1111).

1.title: Engineering Self-Discipline Into Tools
1.tagline: Dora encodes behavioral constraints into her tools because she doesn't trust willpower alone to maintain her standards.
1.insight: Dora recognizes a recurring tension between moving fast and maintaining quality, and rather than relying on self-discipline or mindset shifts, she encodes behavioral constraints directly into Claude Code's skill files and CLAUDE.md rules. She is building a system that enforces the standards she wants to uphold but knows she sometimes won't in the heat of execution. This suggests she views her own behavioral patterns as bugs to be patched at the infrastructure level, not problems to be solved through awareness or intention alone.
1.context: When Dora is configuring Claude Code skill files or CLAUDE.md rules, especially after recognizing a pattern in her own workflow she wants to change.
supporting_evidence: Dora feels tension between moving fast and maintaining quality, and 'is not just trying to fix a tool; she is trying to fix something about herself' (ID 1118).
Her response — 'I want to do less of this' — is a personal, evaluative statement about a habit she wants to actively change, not merely observe (ID 1119).
She is 'deliberately externalizing constraints on her own behavior by encoding them into the tool's skill files' (ID 1120).

1.title: Structure as a Cognitive Tool
1.tagline: Dora's insistence on format conformity is functional — it keeps her externalized thinking legible to her future self under pressure.
1.insight: When Dora provides exact bash commands and immediately demands reformatting to match a specific structure, it is not perfectionism for its own sake. Because she treats Claude Code as an extension of her working memory, structural consistency across skill files reduces the cognitive overhead of retrieving and trusting information later. Inconsistent formats would force her to re-parse and re-interpret each time, defeating the purpose of externalizing her thinking. The rigidity is functional — it ensures her externalized brain remains legible to her future self under pressure.
1.context: When Dora is defining or reviewing the format of skill files and structured documents in Claude Code, particularly during deliberate build sessions.
supporting_evidence: Dora defines the exact format and immediately requests conformity, 'signaling that format conformity matters to her' (ID 1123).
She treats Claude Code as 'an extension of her own working memory and decision-making system' (ID 1120).
She is 'executing a deliberate, structured build session with clear intent at each step,' suggesting format consistency is part of a broader need for predictability in her system (ID 1122).

1.title: Engineering Solutions for Non-Engineering Problems
1.tagline: Dora applies systematic engineering thinking to her own behavioral habits, but the problem she's solving — impulsive speed over quality — may resist engineered interventions.
1.insight: There is a fundamental contradiction in Dora's approach: she treats her own workflow habits as engineering problems, systematically moving from insights to rules to structured files. Yet the core issue — the pull toward speed over quality — is a deeply contextual, emotionally-driven tendency that emerges under pressure, precisely in moments where she is least likely to consult a skill file or rule. Her approach assumes that encoding the right constraint in the right place will intervene at the right moment, but the very nature of the 'moving fast' habit is that it bypasses deliberate systems. She may be building a perfectly engineered solution for a problem that lives outside the reach of her engineering.
1.context: When Dora is under pressure and tempted to move fast at the expense of quality, or when she is reflecting on whether her tool-based constraints are actually changing her behavior.
supporting_evidence: Dora moves 'systematically from abstract behavioral insights to concrete CLAUDE.md rules to structured skill files, treating her own workflow patterns as engineering problems' (ID 1121).
The core tension is between 'moving fast and maintaining quality standards' — a dynamic, situational behavior, not a static configuration error (ID 1118).

Her statement 'I want to do less of this' suggests emotional recognition that this habit has persisted despite her awareness of it, implying that awareness and documentation alone haven't been sufficient in the past (ID 1119).
